title: Mythology
intro: Mythology questions draw overwhelmingly from the Greco-Roman canon, where the Olympian gods, the Trojan War cycle, and the labors of Heracles account for the single largest share of answerlines. Figures like Zeus, Apollo, Athena, and Odysseus recur constantly, alongside the interlocking genealogies of the Theban and Trojan royal houses and the Argonaut and Perseus cycles. Roman variants of the same gods (Jupiter, Juno, Venus) and the founding legends of Rome, from Aeneas to Romulus and Remus, extend this cluster further.

The second major bloc is Norse mythology, built around Odin, Thor, and Loki and the cosmology of Yggdrasil's nine worlds, culminating in Ragnarok. Egyptian mythology forms a tight, frequently recurring cycle around Osiris, Isis, Horus, and Seth, paired with the solar theology of Ra. Hindu and South Asian mythology is anchored by the two great epics, the Ramayana and Mahabharata, and by Vishnu's avatars and Shiva's family. Celtic and Arthurian material spans Irish heroic cycles like Cu Chulainn's and the medieval Arthurian romance tradition of the Round Table and the Grail quest.

Beyond these five dominant traditions, the canon broadens considerably: Mesopotamian myth (Gilgamesh, Enuma Elish, Inanna's descent), East Asian traditions (Shinto kami, Chinese creation myths, Journey to the West), Mesoamerican and Andean cosmologies (Aztec, Mayan, and Incan), and Slavic and Finnish material (the Kalevala, Baba Yaga) each supply a smaller but consistent stream of answerlines. African, Oceanian, and Native American traditions round out the world-mythology coverage, most often through trickster and creator figures.

Cutting across all of these traditions is a large body of cross-cultural motif answerlines — recurring animals (snakes, dragons, horses), elements (fire, water, thunder), numbers, and story types (flood myths, decapitation, transformation into trees or stars) that questions frequently ask about as common links spanning multiple mythological systems rather than any single tradition.

sections.name: Greek and Roman Mythology
sections.blurb: The largest and most heavily tested cluster: the Olympian pantheon, the Trojan War cycle, the labors of Heracles, and the founding legends of Rome.

entries.note: Greek hero of the Twelve Labors ordered by Eurystheus; son of Zeus and Alcmene; wielded a club and lion pelt.
Greek god of prophecy, music, and archery; twin of Artemis; associated with Delphi and the lyre.
Greek virgin goddess of the hunt and the moon; twin of Apollo; punished Actaeon for seeing her bathe.
Athenian hero who slew the Minotaur in the Labyrinth with help from Ariadne's thread.
Greek hero of the Trojan War, invulnerable but for his heel; slew Hector to avenge Patroclus.
Greek goddess of love and beauty, born from sea foam; wife of Hephaestus, lover of Ares.
Founder of Thebes who slew a dragon and sowed its teeth, producing the Spartoi warriors.
Greek goddess of the harvest whose grief over Persephone's abduction by Hades causes winter.
Greek god of wine and ecstatic ritual; twice-born son of Zeus; associated with the Bacchae and maenads.
Greek god of the sea and earthquakes, wielder of the trident; rival of Athena for patronage of Athens.
Legendary musician who descended to the underworld to retrieve Eurydice but lost her by looking back.
Trojan hero who fled burning Troy and founded the line leading to Rome, per Virgil's Aeneid.
Greek messenger god, guide of souls, and patron of thieves and travelers; born on Mount Cyllene.
Phrygian mother goddess adopted into Greco-Roman cult, associated with lions and the priest Attis.
Roman two-faced god of beginnings, doorways, and transitions; namesake of January.
Greek goat-legged god of shepherds and wild places, inventor of the pan-pipes; pursued the nymph Syrinx.
Greek hero who beheaded Medusa and rescued Andromeda from a sea monster.
Greek god of medicine bearing a snake-entwined staff; resurrected the dead and was killed by Zeus for it.
Sorceress who transformed Odysseus's men into swine on her island Aeaea.
Cunning Greek hero of the Odyssey whose ten-year journey home included Circe, Polyphemus, and the Sirens.
King of the Greek gods, wielder of the thunderbolt, notorious for shapeshifting affairs.
Greek goddess of wisdom and war born fully armed from Zeus's head; patron of Athens.
Greek goddess of magic, crossroads, and the night, often depicted with three forms or torches.
Grandson of Zeus who won Hippodamia in a chariot race after his father Tantalus served him to the gods.
Greek goddess abducted by Hades to the underworld, whose return with the seasons explains spring.
Legendary tribe of warrior women fought by Heracles and Theseus, led by Penthesilea and Hippolyta.
City founded by Cadmus, site of the Oedipus myth and the Seven Against Thebes.
Greek hero of the Trojan War who wounded Aphrodite and Ares in battle.
Greek lame smith god of fire and craft; husband of Aphrodite, forger of divine weapons.
Swift huntress who joined the Calydonian boar hunt and was outrun only by Hippomenes's golden-apples trick.
Island home of King Minos, the Labyrinth, and the Minotaur.
Greek poet whose Theogony and Works and Days systematized the gods' genealogy and the ages of man.
Colchian sorceress who helped Jason win the Golden Fleece, later killing her own children in revenge.
Beautiful youth beloved of Aphrodite, killed by a boar, whose death and rebirth mirror seasonal myths.
Greek god of war, son of Zeus and Hera, lover of Aphrodite, generally disliked among the gods.
Site of Apollo's oracle where the Pythia delivered prophecies from a sacred chasm.
Leader of the Argonauts who sought the Golden Fleece with help from Medea.
Legendary founder of Rome who, with twin Remus, was raised by a she-wolf.
Greek goddess of the dawn who abducted mortal lovers, including Tithonus.
Greek queen of the gods, wife of Zeus, noted for jealous persecution of his lovers and offspring.
Titaness mother of Apollo and Artemis by Zeus, persecuted by jealous Hera.
Greek trickster king condemned to eternally push a boulder uphill in the underworld.
Blind Theban prophet who lived as both man and woman and advised Oedipus and Odysseus.
City won by Athena over Poseidon in a contest, home to the Parthenon.
Athenian craftsman who built the Labyrinth and fashioned wax wings for his son Icarus.
Queen of Carthage who fell in love with Aeneas and killed herself after he abandoned her.
Phoenician princess abducted by Zeus disguised as a white bull, giving her name to a continent.
Name shared by several figures, including a fisherman turned sea-god and a Trojan War hero.
Roman god of war, father of Romulus and Remus by Rhea Silvia.
Theban king who unknowingly killed his father and married his mother after solving the Sphinx's riddle.
Son of Helios who lost control of the sun chariot and was struck down by Zeus.
Titan who stole fire for humanity and was punished with an eagle eating his liver daily.
River of the underworld by which the gods swore unbreakable oaths.
Roman goddess of the hearth tended by the Vestal Virgins.
Historical conqueror whose legend grew mythic elements, including claimed descent from Zeus-Ammon.
Greek hero who tamed Pegasus and slew the Chimera before falling from the sky for hubris.
Twin heroes (the Dioscuri) born from Leda, one mortal and one divine, who share immortality.
Wise centaur who tutored Achilles, Jason, and Asclepius; gave up immortality to end his pain.
Mycenaean king who assigned Heracles his Twelve Labors.
Three-bodied giant whose cattle Heracles stole as one of his Twelve Labors.
Priestess transformed into a heifer by Zeus to hide her from jealous Hera, tormented by a gadfly.
Greek king punished by being bound to an eternally spinning fiery wheel for attempting to seduce Hera.
Roman queen of the gods, counterpart to Hera, wife of Jupiter.
Roman king of the gods, counterpart to Zeus, wielder of thunderbolts.
Aegean island where the women murdered their husbands and where Hephaestus landed after being cast from Olympus.
Hero of the Calydonian boar hunt whose life was tied to a magic log kept by his mother.
King of Crete who kept the Minotaur in the Labyrinth built by Daedalus.
First mortal woman, given a jar of evils that she loosed upon the world, leaving only hope inside.
Lapith king and friend of Theseus who was trapped in the underworld after attempting to abduct Persephone.
Seven daughters of Atlas transformed into a star cluster to escape Orion's pursuit.
Mortal woman loved by Cupid/Eros who completed impossible tasks for Venus to win immortality.
City besieged for ten years in the Trojan War over Helen, ultimately taken by the wooden horse.
Rutulian king killed by Aeneas in single combat at the end of the Aeneid.
Mycenaean king who led the Greeks at Troy and was murdered by his wife Clytemnestra upon return.
Cretan princess who gave Theseus the thread to escape the Labyrinth, later abandoned on Naxos.
Greek god of the underworld and the dead, abductor of Persephone.
Greatest Trojan warrior, killed by Achilles in revenge for Patroclus's death.
Spartan king whose wife Helen's abduction sparked the Trojan War.
Phrygian king granted the golden touch by Dionysus, later cursed with donkey ears by Apollo.
Achilles's beloved companion whose death at Hector's hands draws Achilles back into battle.
Cyclops blinded by Odysseus, who then curses his journey home to his father Poseidon.
Shapeshifting sea god who must be forcibly held to reveal prophecy, as Menelaus does.
Crew of heroes who sailed with Jason on the Argo to retrieve the Golden Fleece.
Name shared by a Greek city and the hundred-eyed giant guardian slain by Hermes.
Alternate name of Bellerophon, tamer of Pegasus and slayer of the Chimera.
Fire-breathing giant of Roman legend killed by Hercules for stealing his cattle.
North African city ruled by Dido, where Aeneas briefly took refuge in the Aeneid.
Trojan princess cursed by Apollo to give true prophecies that no one believes.
Greek sun god who drives a chariot across the sky; father of Phaethon.
Ethiopian king and Trojan War ally killed by Achilles, mourned by his mother Eos.
Nine daughters of Zeus and Mnemosyne who inspire poetry, music, and the arts.
Wise elderly king of Pylos who offers counsel to the Greeks at Troy.
Son of Agamemnon who avenges his father by killing his mother Clytemnestra, then is pursued by the Furies.
Giant hunter placed among the stars, associated with pursuit of the Pleiades and death by Artemis or a scorpion.
Euripides play in which Dionysus destroys Thebes's King Pentheus for denying his divinity.
Three goddesses (Moirai) who spin, measure, and cut the thread of every mortal life.
Monstrous serpentine giant who challenged Zeus for control of the cosmos and fathered many monsters.
Hesiod's poem describing the Ages of Man and practical farming wisdom.
Greek tragedian who dramatized myths like the Oresteia and Prometheus Bound.
Trojan prince loved by Aphrodite, father of Aeneas whom Aeneas carries from burning Troy.
Legendary advanced island civilization described by Plato that sank beneath the sea.
Greek god of the north wind who abducted the princess Oreithyia.
Kingdom on the Black Sea where the Golden Fleece hung, ruled by King Aeetes.
Secret Greek rites at Eleusis honoring Demeter and Persephone's myth.
Satyr flayed alive by Apollo after losing a musical contest.
Invulnerable-skinned lion strangled by Heracles in his first labor.
Achilles's son who fought at Troy and killed King Priam.
Trojan prince whose judgment awarding Aphrodite the golden apple and abduction of Helen caused the Trojan War.
Odysseus's faithful wife who fends off suitors for twenty years by unweaving her loom work nightly.
First Greek to die at Troy, whose wife's grief led the gods to briefly revive him.
Italic people whose women were abducted by Romulus's men to populate early Rome.
Six-headed sea monster who preys on sailors passing through a strait opposite Charybdis.
King punished in the underworld with eternally out-of-reach food and water after serving his son Pelops to the gods.
Odysseus's son who searches for news of his father and helps him defeat the suitors.
Mortal taught agriculture by Demeter and sent to spread it across the world.
Greek river god who wrestled Heracles for Deianira, losing a horn in the fight.
Massive Greek warrior at Troy who went mad and killed himself after losing Achilles's armor to Odysseus.
Rustic Greek region associated with Pan and pastoral myth.
Son of Aeneas said to have founded Alba Longa, ancestor city of Rome.
Titan condemned to hold up the sky as punishment for the Titanomachy.
Elderly couple who hosted disguised gods hospitably and were rewarded with transformation into trees.
Titan king who overthrew his father Uranus and was in turn overthrown by his son Zeus.
One-eyed giant race, including Polyphemus, blinded by Odysseus.
Goddess of discord whose golden apple of contention triggered the Judgment of Paris.
Pre-Roman Italian people whose religious practices influenced early Roman mythology and augury.
Chthonic goddesses of vengeance (Erinyes) who pursue those who commit kin-murder, like Orestes.
Primordial Greek earth goddess, mother of the Titans and many monsters.
Trojan youth abducted by Zeus to become cupbearer of the gods.
Trojan prince and prophet, brother of Paris and Cassandra.
Son of Theseus falsely accused by his stepmother Phaedra, cursed and killed by his father.
Beautiful youth loved by Apollo, killed by a discus and transformed into a flower.
Legendary land of eternal spring beyond the north wind, home to a blessed people.
Child-devouring monster of Greek myth, once a mortal woman cursed by Hera.
Thessalian people whose battle with the centaurs at Pirithous's wedding is a famous myth.
Roman household guardian spirits worshipped at the domestic hearth.
Mountain near Troy where Paris made his judgment and Zeus watched the war.
Home of the twelve Olympian gods atop Greece's highest peak.
Greek goddess of retribution who punishes hubris and excess.
Titan personifying the great river encircling the world.
Mortal king who married the sea nymph Thetis; father of Achilles.
Amazon queen who fought at Troy and was killed by Achilles.
Roman goddess of fruit trees and orchards, wooed by Vertumnus.
Roman god of time and harvest, counterpart to Cronus, presiding over the Golden Age.
Deep abyss below the underworld where the Titans were imprisoned.
Greek archer at Troy, half-brother of Ajax the Greater.
Titaness of divine law and order, mother of the Fates and Horae.
Sea nymph mother of Achilles who dipped him in the River Styx.
Mycenaean king unknowingly fed his own children by his brother Atreus.
Elder generation of Greek gods overthrown by Zeus and the Olympians in the Titanomachy.
Island home of the sorceress Circe.
King of Colchis who guarded the Golden Fleece, father of Medea.
Ethiopian princess chained to a rock as a sacrifice, rescued by Perseus from a sea monster.
Ship built for Jason and the Argonauts' quest for the Golden Fleece.
Nymph who detained Odysseus on her island Ogygia for seven years.
Mortal hunter loved by Eos, accidentally killed his wife Procris with his infallible spear.
Three-headed hound guarding the gates of the underworld, captured by Heracles in his final labor.
Primordial void of Greek cosmogony from which Gaia, Tartarus, and Eros emerged.
Fire-breathing hybrid monster (lion, goat, serpent) slain by Bellerophon.
Nymph transformed into a laurel tree to escape Apollo's pursuit.
Sacred island birthplace of Apollo and Artemis.
Ancient oracle of Zeus where priests interpreted the rustling of sacred oak leaves.
Greek god of desire, son of Aphrodite, lover of Psyche.
Roman goddess of luck and fate, often depicted with a wheel.
Three snake-haired sisters, including Medusa, whose gaze turns onlookers to stone.
Greek goddess of youth, cupbearer of the gods, wife of Heracles after his apotheosis.
Roman name for Heracles, hero of the Twelve Labors.
Greek goddess personifying the rainbow and messenger of the gods.
Trojan king who cheated Apollo and Poseidon of payment for building Troy's walls.
Frenzied female followers of Dionysus who tear apart victims like Pentheus and Orpheus.
Greek seer who gained the ability to understand animals after snakes licked his ears.
Magical herb given by Hermes to Odysseus to resist Circe's transformation spell.
Warrior people led by Achilles at Troy, said to be born from ants.
Theban queen whose boasting over her many children led Apollo and Artemis to kill them all.
Greek hero who exposed Odysseus's feigned madness, later framed and killed by him in revenge.
Sacred statue of Athena whose possession was believed to protect Troy.
Cretan queen who mated with a bull to produce the Minotaur.
Usurper king of Iolcus who sent Jason to fetch the Golden Fleece.
Daedalus's nephew, inventor of the saw, thrown off a cliff by his jealous uncle and turned into a partridge.
Aged king of Troy who begs Achilles for the return of Hector's body.
Minor Greek fertility god depicted with exaggerated proportions, protector of gardens.
Roman deified form of Romulus, worshipped as a war and state god.
Vestal Virgin mother of Romulus and Remus by the god Mars.
Modern author of "The Greek Myths" and "The White Goddess," influential mythography.
Mortal mother of Dionysus, incinerated upon seeing Zeus's true divine form.
Failed expedition of seven champions to install Polynices as king of Thebes.
Bird-women whose irresistible song lures sailors to shipwreck, evaded by Odysseus.
Riddling monster outside Thebes defeated by Oedipus's answer about man.
Trojan prince granted immortality but not eternal youth by his lover Eos, withering forever.
Roman goddess of love and beauty, counterpart to Aphrodite, mother of Aeneas.
Roman smith god of fire, counterpart to Hephaestus.
Hunter transformed into a stag by Artemis after seeing her bathe, then torn apart by his own hounds.
Greek keeper of the winds who gives Odysseus a bag containing all but the west wind.
Lesser Greek warrior at Troy who assaulted Cassandra, drawing divine punishment on the fleet.
Wife who agreed to die in her husband Admetus's place, later rescued from death by Heracles.
Theban general whose form Zeus assumed to seduce Alcmene, fathering Heracles.
Hector's wife, enslaved after Troy's fall and mourner of her husband and son.
Giant wrestler invincible while touching the earth, defeated by Heracles lifting him aloft.
Twin children of Zeus and Leto, gods of the sun/prophecy and moon/hunt respectively.
Nymph transformed into a spring to escape the river god Alpheus's pursuit.
Poet saved from drowning by a dolphin after being thrown overboard by pirates; also an immortal talking horse.
Epic convention of a hero's finest moment of glory in battle, as with Diomedes in the Iliad.
Phrygian consort of Cybele who castrated himself and died, later resurrected in her cult.
Master thief and grandfather of Odysseus, gifted with cunning by his father Hermes.
Herald's staff of Hermes entwined with two snakes.
Monstrous boar sent by Artemis to ravage Calydon, hunted down by Atalanta and Meleager.
Famous hunt uniting Greek heroes to kill the boar sent by Artemis, ending in Meleager's death.
Horn of plenty broken from the goat Amalthea, symbol of endless abundance.
Theban king who condemns Antigone for burying her brother against his edict.
Roman god of desire, son of Venus, lover of Psyche (counterpart to Eros).
One-eyed giants who forge Zeus's thunderbolts and include Polyphemus.
Wife of Heracles who unwittingly poisons him with the blood of the centaur Nessus.
Half-woman, half-serpent "Mother of Monsters" who bore the Chimera, Cerberus, and other creatures.
Nymph cursed to only repeat others' words, who wastes away pining for Narcissus.
Kingdom of Andromeda's parents Cepheus and Cassiopeia in Perseus's myth.
Orpheus's wife, fatally lost when he looks back while leading her from the underworld.
Athletic contests held in honor of the dead, e.g. those for Patroclus in the Iliad.
War between the Olympian gods and the earth-born Giants.
Fleece of a magical ram sought by Jason and the Argonauts from Colchis.
Wife of Cadmus, given a cursed necklace that brought misfortune to her descendants.
Most beautiful woman in the world, wife of Menelaus, whose abduction by Paris sparked the Trojan War.
Most beautiful woman in the world, whose abduction by Paris sparked the Trojan War.
Amazon queen whose girdle Heracles was tasked to obtain.
Greek personification of sleep, brother of Thanatos (death).
Son of Daedalus who flew too close to the sun on wax wings and fell into the sea.
Odysseus's home island kingdom, awaited by Penelope for twenty years.
King of Thebes and father of Oedipus, killed by his son at a crossroads in fulfillment of prophecy.
Arcadian king transformed into a wolf by Zeus for serving him human flesh.
Thracian king driven mad and punished for opposing the cult of Dionysus.
Mountain where the infant Oedipus was left to die and where Pentheus was torn apart by maenads.
Kingdom of Agamemnon, whose royal house is cursed across generations.
Princess transformed into a myrrh tree after an incestuous union that produced Adonis.
Trojan companions in the Aeneid whose devoted friendship ends in a doomed night raid.
Legendary second king of Rome credited with founding its religious institutions.
Winged horse born from Medusa's blood, tamed and ridden by Bellerophon.
Greek archer abandoned on Lemnos with a festering wound, later recalled to Troy for his bow.
Princess raped and mutilated by Tereus, transformed into a nightingale after her revenge.
Boy carried to Colchis on the golden ram whose fleece became the Golden Fleece.
Twin founders of Rome raised by a she-wolf after being abandoned as infants.
Son of Zeus and Trojan ally killed by Patroclus, his body carried home by Sleep and Death.
Warriors sprung from dragon's teeth sown by Cadmus at the founding of Thebes.
Man-eating birds with metallic feathers driven off by Heracles in one of his labors.
Bronze automaton guardian of Crete slain by the Argonauts through his single vulnerable vein.
Roman maiden who betrayed the citadel to the Sabines and was crushed by their shields as payment.
Argonaut and father of Ajax the Greater, participant in the Calydonian boar hunt.
Mysterious metalworking beings of Rhodes credited with early bronze craftsmanship.
Son of Odysseus and Circe who unknowingly kills his father.
Uncatchable fox sent to plague Thebes, resolved when Zeus turned both it and its pursuing hound to stone.
Robert Graves's mythographic study proposing a unifying poetic moon-goddess myth.
Hesiod's poem systematizing the birth and genealogy of the Greek gods.
Ugly, insubordinate Greek soldier at Troy killed by Achilles for mocking Penthesilea.
Region associated with Orpheus, Ares, and the god Dionysus's northern cult.
War between the Olympian gods and the Titans, ending in Zeus's victory.
Merman son of Poseidon and Amphitrite who calms the seas with his conch shell.
Ten-year Greek siege of Troy sparked by Paris's abduction of Helen.
King whose wife Alcestis agreed to die in his place, later rescued from death by Heracles.
Son of Zeus made a judge of the dead for his renowned justice in life.
King of Athens and father of Theseus, who drowned himself believing his son had died.
Nymph and island named for her, mother of Aeacus by Zeus.
Protective shield or breastplate of Zeus and Athena, often bearing the Gorgon's head.
City founded by Ascanius, ancestral to Rome and birthplace of Romulus and Remus's mother.
Modern occultist whose syncretic mysticism drew heavily on Greco-Egyptian mythological symbolism.
Theban king whose magical lyre-playing moved stones into place to build the city's walls.
Sea goddess wife of Poseidon, mother of Triton.
Daughter of Oedipus condemned by Creon for burying her brother Polynices against his edict.
Amazon queen abducted by Theseus, mother of Hippolytus.
Minor god of beekeeping and agriculture whose pursuit of Eurydice indirectly caused her death.
Athenian comic playwright whose plays like The Birds and The Frogs draw on mythological themes.
Greek goddess personifying mischief, delusion, and ruinous folly.
King of Boeotia driven mad by Hera, whose children Phrixus and Helle fled on a golden ram.
Mycenaean king who fed his brother Thyestes's own children to him, cursing the House of Atreus.
Captive woman whose seizure by Agamemnon sparks Achilles's wrath in the Iliad.
Greek seer at Troy who advises Agamemnon to sacrifice Iphigenia for favorable winds.
Nymph transformed into a bear by a jealous Hera after being seduced by Zeus.
Warrior maiden ally of Turnus in the Aeneid, raised in the wild and devoted to Diana.
Bandit king who forced travelers to wrestle him to the death, defeated by Theseus.
Ferryman who carries the dead across the River Styx for a coin's fare.
City where Medea took refuge with Jason before he abandoned her for the princess Glauce.
Rampaging bull captured by Heracles and later slain by Theseus at Marathon.
Island birthplace of Aphrodite, who emerged from the sea foam nearby.
Fifty daughters of Danaus who murdered their husbands, punished in the underworld with endless water-carrying.
Greek flood survivor who repopulates the earth with Pyrrha by throwing stones behind them.
Greek goddess of childbirth, daughter of Zeus and Hera.
Site of the Eleusinian Mysteries honoring Demeter and Persephone's myth.
Blessed afterlife realm reserved for heroes and the virtuous dead.
Greek sanctuary and healing center dedicated to Asclepius.
Titan brother of Prometheus who accepted Pandora as a gift despite warnings.
Primordial Greek goddess sometimes credited with dancing the world into creation.
Arcadian founder of a settlement on the future site of Rome who allies with Aeneas.
Ivory statue brought to life by Aphrodite after the sculptor Pygmalion fell in love with it.
Roman guardian spirit believed to accompany each person or place through life.
Hundred-handed giants who aid Zeus in the Titanomachy.
Greek goddess of the hearth and home, eldest of the Olympian siblings.
Princess won by Pelops in a rigged chariot race against her father Oenomaus.
Legendary Greek poet credited with the Iliad and Odyssey, foundational epics of the Trojan War cycle.
Argonaut youth abducted by water nymphs while fetching water, causing Heracles to abandon the quest.
Theban queen who raised the infant Dionysus and later became the sea goddess Leucothea.
Girl raised as a boy and transformed into a man by Isis so she could marry her betrothed.
Serpent-dragon guarding the golden apples of the Hesperides, slain by Heracles.
Cannibal giants who destroy most of Odysseus's fleet.
Latin princess betrothed to Turnus but married to Aeneas, whose war she indirectly causes.
Snake-haired Gorgon whose gaze turns onlookers to stone, beheaded by Perseus.
Severed Gorgon head retaining its petrifying power, later mounted on Athena's aegis.
Theban defender killed in the war of the Seven Against Thebes.
Ovid's narrative poem retelling numerous Greco-Roman transformation myths.
Bull-headed monster born of Pasiphae, kept in the Labyrinth and slain by Theseus.
Sicilian volcano said to be the prison of the monster Typhon or the forge of Hephaestus.
Mountain sacred to Apollo and the Muses, near Delphi.
Chariot-driver whose sabotage helps Pelops win Hippodamia, then is betrayed and cursed by Pelops.
Cursed jewelry given to Cadmus's wife Harmonia that brought misfortune to their descendants.
Site where Heracles slew the invulnerable Nemean Lion in his first labor.
Fifty sea-nymph daughters of Nereus, including Thetis, mother of Achilles.
Island home of the nymph Calypso, where she detained Odysseus for seven years.
Two-headed dog guarding Geryon's cattle, killed by Heracles.
One of Rome's seven hills, traditional site of the city's founding by Romulus.
Primordial deity of the Orphic cosmogony who hatches from the cosmic egg to begin creation.
Blind prophet-king tormented by harpies until rescued by the Argonauts.
Primordial sea god, father of monstrous children including the Gorgons and Graeae.
Anatolian kingdom associated with Midas, Cybele, and Attis.
Athenian philosopher whose dialogues preserve the myth of Atlantis and the Myth of Er.
Roman god of the underworld and the dead, counterpart to Hades.
Wife of Cephalus accidentally killed by his infallible spear out of jealous suspicion.
Bandit who forced travelers onto an iron bed, stretching or cutting them to fit, defeated by Theseus.
Kingdom of the wise elder Nestor in the Trojan War cycle.
Twin brother of Romulus, killed by him in a dispute over founding Rome.
Titaness mother of Zeus who saved him from being devoured by Cronus.
Island associated with the Cyclopes, Mount Etna's Typhon, and Persephone's abduction.
Late Roman sun god cult promoted as unifying state religion before Christianity.
Screech-owl-like vampiric creature of Roman folklore said to prey on infants.
Wounded king healed by the same spear that injured him, per Achilles's rust.
Thracian bard blinded and silenced by the Muses for boasting he could surpass them.
Greek personification of death, brother of Hypnos (sleep).
Virgil's epic of Aeneas's journey from Troy to found the line leading to Rome.
Apollonius of Rhodes's epic recounting Jason and the Argonauts' quest for the Golden Fleece.
Twin heroes Castor and Pollux, born from Leda, one mortal and one divine, who share immortality.
Euripides tragedy depicting the suffering of Trojan women after the fall of Troy.
Greek women's festival honoring Demeter and Persephone tied to fertility and the harvest.
Wooden horse used by the Greeks to smuggle soldiers into Troy and end the war.
Fierce warrior among the Seven Against Thebes, father of Diomedes.
Roman god of seasons and change who woos Pomona through shapeshifting disguises.
Roman poet who authored the Aeneid, recounting Aeneas's journey to found Rome's lineage.
Orphic form of Dionysus, torn apart by Titans and reborn.
Body parts stolen by the monster Typhon and later recovered by Hermes and Pan.

sections.name: Norse and Germanic Mythology
sections.blurb: Odin, Thor, and Loki preside over the nine realms of Yggdrasil, building toward the apocalyptic battle of Ragnarok.

entries.note: Norse All-Father, god of war, wisdom, and poetry; sacrificed an eye at Mimir's well and hung from Yggdrasil for runes.
Norse trickster god and shapeshifter, eventual father of monsters; bound after orchestrating Baldr's death.
Norse thunder god wielding the hammer Mjolnir; son of Odin, protector of Asgard against giants.
Norse world tree connecting the nine realms, gnawed at its roots by the serpent Nidhoggr.
Norse Vanir god of fertility and harvest who traded his sword for the giantess Gerd.
Norse god of beauty and light killed by a mistletoe dart thrown by the blind Hodr at Loki's urging.
Norse apocalyptic battle in which most gods die and the world is remade.
Norse Vanir goddess of love and war who owns the necklace Brisingamen and a falcon-feather cloak.
Norse dragon-slayer hero who killed Fafnir and gained a cursed treasure hoard.
Norse primordial giant whose slain body formed the earth, sea, and sky.
Norse god of poetry and eloquence, husband of Idunn.
Wisest of the Norse gods, killed by dwarves who brewed his blood into the mead of poetry.
Norse watchman god of the Bifrost bridge who will sound his horn at Ragnarok.
Norse giantess of winter and skiing who married Njord after avenging her father.
Norse god of war and law who sacrificed his hand to bind the wolf Fenrir.
Giant king who tricks Thor and Loki through illusions during a visit to his hall.
Norse female spirits who choose slain warriors to carry to Valhalla.
Norse necklace of Freyja, forged by dwarves and later stolen by Loki.
Norse dwarf-turned-dragon slain by Sigurd for his cursed gold hoard.
Alternate spelling of Heimdall, watchman god of the Bifrost bridge.
Stone-hearted giant defeated by Thor in a boasting contest turned duel.
Thor's hammer, forged by dwarves, capable of leveling mountains and always returning to his hand.
Odin's eight-legged horse, born of Loki disguised as a mare.
Norse realm of the Aesir gods, connected to Midgard by the Bifrost bridge.
Norse craftsman race who forged treasures like Mjolnir and Draupnir.
Norse craftsman race who forged treasures like Mjolnir and Draupnir.
Norse goddess of love and war owning the necklace Brisingamen (variant spelling of Freyja).
Odin's wife and queen of Asgard, associated with foresight and motherhood (variant of Frigg).
Legendary Swedish king who questions the gods in the frame narrative of the Prose Edda's Gylfaginning.
Norse goddess ruling the underworld realm of the same name, daughter of Loki.
Norse magical alphabet discovered by Odin through self-sacrifice on Yggdrasil.
Thor's golden-haired wife whose hair was cut by Loki and replaced by dwarf-forged gold.
Cursed magic sword of Norse legend that must draw blood whenever drawn.
Norse giant/god of the sea who brews ale for the gods.
Slain warriors chosen by valkyries to feast and train in Valhalla for Ragnarok.
Norse World Serpent encircling the earth, fated to kill and be killed by Thor at Ragnarok.
Magical brew from Kvasir's blood granting the gift of poetry to whoever drinks it.
Norse being of wisdom whose severed head Odin consults; namesake of the well where Odin sacrificed an eye.
Spectral procession of huntsmen and hounds across the sky, an omen of disaster in Germanic folklore.
Norse fertility-associated race of gods, including Freyr and Freyja, who war with then join the Aesir.
Legendary Norse clan whose saga includes Sigurd's dragon-slaying and cursed treasure.
Historical king mythologized in Germanic legend (Atli) as the treacherous husband of Gudrun.
Norse god of beauty and light killed by a mistletoe dart (variant spelling of Baldr).
Norse warriors said to fight in a trance-like fury, sometimes linked to wolf or bear totems.
Norse Vanir god of fertility and harvest (variant spelling of Freyr).
Section of the Prose Edda recounting the gods' stories through King Gylfi's questioning.
Setting where the Eddas were compiled, preserving Norse mythology.
Germanic household or mine spirits, sometimes mischievous, related to dwarf/gnome folklore.
Thor's hammer, forged by dwarves (variant spelling of Mjolnir).
Norse serpent that gnaws at the roots of Yggdrasil.
Norse Vanir god of the sea and wealth, father of Freyr and Freyja, husband of Skadi.
Three Norse female beings who weave the fates of gods and mortals at the well of Urd.
Icelandic prose compilation by Snorri Sturluson recording Norse myths, including the Gylfaginning.
Volsung hero who draws a sword from a tree trunk, father of the dragon-slayer Sigurd.
Norse god associated with archery, skiing, and winter, little detailed in surviving sources.
City of the Nibelungenlied, the medieval German epic drawing on the Volsung/Sigurd legend.
Rainbow bridge connecting Asgard to Midgard, guarded by Heimdall.
Valkyrie-turned-mortal betrothed to Sigurd, whose betrayal drives the Volsung tragedy.
Germanic legendary hero based on Theodoric the Great, featured in the Nibelungenlied cycle.
The Prose and Poetic Eddas, primary Icelandic sources for Norse mythology.
Norse supernatural beings associated with beauty, magic, and light in Germanic folklore.
Odin's wife and queen of Asgard, associated with foresight and motherhood.
Giant who tries to kill Thor with a hot iron rod, defeated with the help of Grid's staff.
Unbreakable ribbon-like fetter made by dwarves to bind the wolf Fenrir.
Sigurd's sword, reforged from the shards of his father Sigmund's broken blade, used to slay Fafnir.
Mysterious Norse figure whose burning by the Aesir sparks the war between Aesir and Vanir.
Norse underworld realm ruled by the goddess Hel.
Norse god who helps Odin and Lodurr create the first humans, Ask and Embla.
Norse goddess who guards the golden apples of youth, briefly stolen by a giant with Loki's help.
Norse couple who survive Ragnarok hidden in Yggdrasil to repopulate the world.
Norse legendary archer-hero prophesied to die from his own horse's skull.
Fire giant destined to battle the gods and set the world ablaze at Ragnarok.
Giant who abducts Idunn and her apples of youth, killed by the gods in pursuit.
Giant who steals Thor's hammer Mjolnir and demands Freyja as ransom.
Wise giant who loses a contest of cosmological knowledge to a disguised Odin.
Odin's hall in Asgard where slain warriors feast, awaiting Ragnarok.
Norse god born solely to avenge Baldr's death by killing Hodr.
Legendary Norse smith mutilated and enslaved by a king, who takes brutal revenge and flies away.
Fortifications built by a disguised giant in exchange for Freyja, the sun, and the moon, foiled by Loki.
Legendary Germanic smith mutilated and enslaved by a king, who takes brutal revenge and flies away.

sections.name: Celtic and Arthurian Legend
sections.blurb: Irish, Welsh, and British cycles spanning the Ulster Cycle's Cu Chulainn, the Mabinogion, and the Knights of the Round Table.

entries.note: Homeland of the Mabinogion tales and figures like Gwydion, Pryderi, and Rhiannon.
Setting of Irish mythological cycles featuring Cu Chulainn, the Tuatha De Danann, and the Morrigan.
Irish hero of the Ulster Cycle known for his battle-frenzy and single-handed defense of Ulster.
Legendary British king who drew the sword from the stone and led the Knights of the Round Table.
Irish hero of the Ulster Cycle noted for his battle-frenzy (variant spelling of Cu Chulainn).
Welsh horse-associated goddess figure from the Mabinogion, falsely accused of killing her son.
Wizard and advisor of King Arthur, said to have arranged his conception and built Stonehenge.
King Arthur's traitorous son (or nephew) who fatally wounds him at the Battle of Camlann.
Knight of the Round Table who beheads the Green Knight and honors a return blow a year later.
Giant Welsh king of the Mabinogion whose severed head continued speaking and protecting Britain.
Irish hero (Finn McCool) who led the Fianna warrior band and gained wisdom from the Salmon of Knowledge.
Irish god of many skills who led the Tuatha De Danann against the Fomorians.
Collection of medieval Welsh mythological tales featuring Pryderi, Rhiannon, and Bran.
Irish goddess of war and fate, often appearing as a crow to foretell death.
Mystical island where the wounded King Arthur is taken to be healed.
Welsh hero of the Mabinogion, son of Pwyll and Rhiannon, whose disappearance and return frame several branches.
Purest Knight of the Round Table, sole achiever of the Holy Grail quest.
Legendary Welsh bard said to possess supernatural wisdom from Ceridwen's cauldron.
Final battle in which King Arthur and Mordred mortally wound each other.
Irish hero (Fionn mac Cumhaill) who led the Fianna and gained wisdom from the Salmon of Knowledge.
Region linked to Arthurian legend and the forest of Broceliande.
Queen of Camelot, wife of King Arthur, whose affair with Lancelot fractures the Round Table.
King Arthur's brusque foster brother and seneschal of the Round Table.
Greatest Knight of the Round Table, whose affair with Guinevere dooms Camelot.
Irish province defended by Cu Chulainn in the Ulster Cycle.
Frankish emperor whose legendary court (paladins like Roland) parallels Arthurian romance cycles.
Priestly class of ancient Celtic society associated with sacred groves and ritual.
Knight of the Round Table who beheads the Green Knight and honors the return blow.
Greatest Knight of the Round Table, whose affair with Guinevere fractures Camelot.
Irish sea god who rules the Otherworld island of Emain Ablach.
Enchantress and half-sister of King Arthur, sometimes his healer, sometimes his foe.
Legendary paladin of Charlemagne who dies covering the retreat at Roncevaux, blowing his horn Oliphant.
Warrior-woman who trains Cu Chulainn in combat on the Isle of Skye.
Knight of the Round Table who survives the Grail quest alongside Galahad and Percival.
Welsh Otherworld ruled by Arawn, featured in the Mabinogion.
Irish tragic heroine whose beauty and doomed love bring ruin to Ulster.
Irish heroine transformed into a fly and reborn, central to the Tochmarc Etaine tale.
King Arthur's magical sword, sometimes said to be given by the Lady of the Lake.
Irish board game of strategy featured in mythological tales as a wager between heroes and gods.
Monstrous race opposing the Tuatha De Danann in Irish mythology.
Welsh magician of the Mabinogion who helps create Blodeuwedd, a woman made of flowers.
Biblical figure said to have brought the Holy Grail to Britain, founding the Grail legend.
Mystical figure who gives Excalibur to King Arthur and fosters Lancelot.
Irish fairy folk known as shoemakers who guard hidden pots of gold.
Warrior-queen of Connacht in the Ulster Cycle who launches the Cattle Raid of Cooley against Cu Chulainn.
Irish poet-hero, son of Fionn mac Cumhaill, who travels to the Otherworld Tir na nOg.
English legendary outlaw of Sherwood Forest who robs from the rich to give to the poor.
Setting for Gaelic legendary material and figures like Scathach's Isle of Skye.
Tragic Arthurian knight who deals the Dolorous Stroke, wounding the Fisher King.
Irish "good god" of the Tuatha De Danann, owner of a magic cauldron and club.
Sacred vessel sought by the Knights of the Round Table, achieved only by the pure Galahad.
Cornish knight whose tragic love for Iseult parallels the Lancelot-Guinevere affair.
Legendary sunken city of Breton legend, drowned due to its princess's sin.
Knight of the Round Table in Chretien de Troyes's romance, the "Knight of the Lion."
Irish spirit woman whose wail foretells a death in the family she visits.
Irish goddess of fire, poetry, and healing, later syncretized with Saint Brigid.
Irish druid of the Ulster Cycle who prophesies over Deirdre and Cu Chulainn.
Welsh hero of the Mabinogion tale "Culhwch and Olwen," set impossible tasks to win his bride.
Irish tragic heroine whose beauty and doomed love bring ruin to Ulster.
Arthurian maiden who tricks Lancelot into fathering Galahad.
Gallo-Roman Celtic horse goddess protecting horses and their riders.
Supernatural folk of Celtic tradition, dwelling in the Otherworld and interacting with mortals.
Irish hero (Fionn mac Cumhaill) who led the Fianna and gained wisdom from the Salmon of Knowledge.
Cu Chulainn's barbed spear, a gift from his teacher Scathach, fatal in a single strike.
Purest Knight of the Round Table, sole achiever of the Holy Grail quest.
Irish princess betrothed to Fionn mac Cumhaill who elopes with the younger Diarmuid.
Welsh magician of the Mabinogion who helps create Blodeuwedd, a woman made of flowers.
Biblical evangelist sometimes conflated with the Grail legend's Fisher King tradition.
Legendary British king who drew the sword from the stone and led the Knights of the Round Table.
Arthurian knight famed for his endless pursuit of the Questing Beast.
Bizarre composite monster endlessly pursued by King Pellinore in Arthurian legend.
Christian warrior-saint famed for slaying a dragon to save a princess.
Patron saint of Ireland credited with driving snakes from the island and converting it to Christianity.
Empty seat at the Round Table reserved for the knight destined to achieve the Holy Grail.
Knight who returns Excalibur to the lake at King Arthur's death.
Cornish knight whose tragic love for Iseult parallels the Lancelot-Guinevere affair.
Cornish castle traditionally cited as King Arthur's birthplace.
Divine race of Irish mythology who battle the Fomorians for control of Ireland.
King Arthur's father, who conceives him with Igraine through Merlin's magic.
Legendary Swiss marksman forced to shoot an apple off his son's head.

sections.name: Egyptian Mythology
sections.blurb: The Osiris-Isis-Horus-Seth cycle and the solar theology of Ra anchor a pantheon tied to the Nile, kingship, and the afterlife.

entries.note: Egyptian falcon-headed sky god, son of Osiris and Isis, who avenged his father against Seth.
Egyptian ibis-headed god of writing and wisdom who recorded judgment of the dead.
Egyptian god of the underworld and resurrection, murdered and dismembered by Seth, restored by Isis.
Egyptian goddess of magic who reassembled Osiris's body and protected her son Horus.
Egyptian sun god who sails across the sky by day and battles the serpent Apep by night.
Egyptian creator god of Memphis associated with craftsmen, who thought and spoke the world into being.
Egyptian crocodile-headed god associated with the Nile's power and fertility.
Homeland of the Osiris myth cycle and the pantheon of Ra, Isis, and Horus.
Ram-headed Egyptian creator god who molded humans on a potter's wheel.
Egyptian jackal-headed god of embalming who weighs the hearts of the dead.
Egyptian serpent of chaos who battles Ra nightly to prevent the sun's rising.
Egyptian cow-goddess of love, music, and motherhood.
Egyptian creator god later merged with Ra (Amun-Ra) as king of the gods at Thebes.
Egyptian moon god, son of Amun and Mut, part of the Theban triad.
Greco-Egyptian syncretic god combining Osiris and Apis, promoted under the Ptolemies.
Egyptian god of chaos and the desert who murdered his brother Osiris.
Egyptian cat goddess of home and protection.
Egyptian underworld through which the dead journey and Ra sails at night.
Annual event mythologized as tied to Osiris's death and Isis's tears.
Egyptian cult center of the sun god Ra/Atum and the Ennead.
Egyptian scarab-headed god representing the rising sun and rebirth.
Egyptian creator and war goddess associated with weaving and the city of Sais.
Egyptian sky goddess who arches over the earth god Geb, swallowing the sun each night.
Egyptian lioness war goddess who nearly destroyed humanity before being tricked into drunkenness.
Egyptian god of chaos who murdered his brother Osiris and fought Horus for the throne.
Sacred bull worshipped as a manifestation of Ptah at Memphis.
Egyptian funerary vessels storing a mummy's organs under the protection of Horus's four sons.
Middle Kingdom funerary spells inscribed on coffins guiding the dead through the afterlife.
Sacred and feared animal associated with the god Sobek.
Ancient Egyptian capital and cult center of the creator god Ptah.
Egyptian personification of the primordial watery chaos from which creation arose.
Eight primordial deities of Hermopolis representing the chaotic forces before creation.
Egyptian god of air who separates the sky goddess Nut from the earth god Geb.
Egyptian hippopotamus goddess protecting women in childbirth.
Syncretic combination of the creator god Amun and sun god Ra as king of the Egyptian gods.
Homeland of the Osiris myth cycle and the pantheon of Ra, Isis, and Horus.
Egyptian sun-disk deity elevated to sole god during Akhenaten's monotheistic reforms.
Egyptian creator god of Heliopolis who self-generated and produced the first divine pair.
Egyptian cat goddess of home and protection (variant of Bastet).
Greek name for the child-god Horus, depicted with a finger to his lips as god of silence.
Egyptian goddess and concept of truth and cosmic order against whose feather hearts are weighed.
Egyptian goddess, sister of Isis and Osiris, associated with mourning and protection of the dead.
Sacred and feared animal associated with the god Sobek.
Life-giving river whose annual flooding was mythologized as tied to Osiris and Isis.
Four Egyptian funerary deities protecting the mummy's organs in canopic jars.
Egyptian cobra goddess and protector of Lower Egypt, depicted on the pharaoh's crown.

sections.name: Near Eastern and Mesopotamian Mythology
sections.blurb: Sumerian, Babylonian, Canaanite, and Persian traditions, from Gilgamesh's quest for immortality to Marduk's defeat of Tiamat.

entries.note: Sumerian king-hero of Uruk who sought immortality after the death of his companion Enkidu.
Mesopotamian goddess of love and war who descended to the underworld to confront her sister Ereshkigal.
Babylonian storm god who slew the primordial Tiamat and created the world from her body.
Babylonian primordial sea goddess slain by Marduk, her body forming heaven and earth.
Sumerian god of water and wisdom who warned Atrahasis of the coming flood.
Sumerian god of wind and storms who sent the flood to punish humanity's noise.
Canaanite storm god who battles the sea god Yam and the death god Mot.
Mesopotamian city associated with Marduk's cult and the Enuma Elish creation epic.
Wild man created to be Gilgamesh's equal and companion; his death spurs Gilgamesh's quest for immortality.
Babylonian creation epic describing Marduk's defeat of Tiamat.
Deity of a Roman-era mystery cult depicted slaying a cosmic bull, with roots in Persian tradition.
Persian hero of the Shahnameh known for his strength and tragic duel with his son Sohrab.
Mesopotamian queen of the underworld who traps her sister Inanna/Ishtar.
Sumerian goddess of love and war who descends to the underworld and is judged by Ereshkigal.
Persian "Book of Kings" epic recounting legendary heroes like Rostam.
Sumerian city ruled by Gilgamesh.
Mesopotamian flood survivor granted immortality, who tells Gilgamesh of the deluge.
Canaanite storm god who battles the sea god Yam and the death god Mot.
Syncretic Greco-Egyptian sage-god (Hermes/Thoth) credited with esoteric hermetic writings.
Phoenician patron god of Tyre, later identified with Heracles.
Mesopotamian warrior god who defeats the monster Anzu and the demon Asag.
Mesopotamian sun god of justice who gives Hammurabi his law code.
Zoroastrian spirit of destruction and chaos opposing Ahura Mazda.
Canaanite warrior goddess, sister and consort of Baal.
Sumerian sky god and head of the pantheon.
Mesopotamian flood-survivor hero of the Atrahasis epic, warned by Enki.
Babylonian storm god who slew Tiamat and created the world, worshipped under the title "Bel" (Lord).
Animated clay figures of Jewish folklore brought to life through sacred words.
Ancient Anatolian civilization whose myths include the storm god Teshub battling the serpent Illuyanka.
Night demon of Jewish folklore, sometimes described as Adam's first, rebellious wife.
Legendary Sumerian king, father of Gilgamesh, hero of his own epic tales.
Sumerian mother/earth goddess who helps create humanity alongside Enki.
Divine tavern-keeper who counsels Gilgamesh to embrace life rather than seek immortality.
Central Asian sky god chief of the Turkic and Mongolic pantheon.
Gloomy land of the dead ruled by Ereshkigal, entered by Inanna in her famous descent.
Mesopotamian lion-headed eagle who steals the Tablet of Destinies and is defeated by Ninurta.
Canaanite storm god who battles the sea god Yam and the death god Mot.
Phoenician coastal city tied to the cult of Adonis and the Osiris myth's Egyptian-Levantine crossover.
Supernatural spirits of Arabian and Islamic folklore capable of good or evil.
Zoroastrian primordial man from whom humanity is said to descend.
Monstrous guardian of the Cedar Forest slain by Gilgamesh and Enkidu.
Homeland of Zoroastrian mythology and the Shahnameh's legendary heroes.
Consort of Tiamat whose blood is used by Marduk to create humanity.
Hurrian god who castrates the sky god Anu, precursor to the Greek Cronus-Uranus myth.
Roman-era mystery cult centered on the god Mithras slaying a cosmic bull.
Babylonian god of writing and wisdom, son of Marduk.
Mesopotamian god of war and plague who becomes co-ruler of the underworld with Ereshkigal.
Mesopotamian demon-king of the wind, paradoxically invoked to protect against other evil spirits.
Title applied to goddesses like Inanna/Ishtar denoting supreme celestial authority.
Legendary ruler who tests Solomon's wisdom in biblical and Ethiopian tradition.
Ancient steppe people whose legendary origins involve Heracles and a serpent-woman.
Mesopotamian artifact granting its holder supreme authority over the gods.
Mesopotamian shepherd-god whose death and return with Ishtar/Inanna marks seasonal renewal.
Carthaginian (Phoenician) goddess of fertility and the moon.
Hittite/Hurrian storm god who battles the serpent Illuyanka.
Sumerian/Babylonian epic of the king who sought immortality after the death of Enkidu.
Ancient Persian religion centered on the cosmic struggle between Ahura Mazda and Ahriman.

sections.name: Hindu and South Asian Mythology
sections.blurb: The Vedic pantheon and the epics Ramayana and Mahabharata, following Vishnu's avatars, Shiva's family, and the Pandava-Kaurava rivalry.

entries.note: Hindu god of destruction and transformation; the cosmic dancer; husband of Parvati, father of Ganesha.
Avatar of Vishnu; charioteer and advisor to Arjuna in the Bhagavad Gita; slayer of the tyrant Kamsa.
Pandava archer-hero of the Mahabharata counseled by Krishna before the Kurukshetra War.
Elephant-headed Hindu god of wisdom and new beginnings; son of Shiva and Parvati.
Vedic king of the gods and storm deity who slew the serpent Vritra.
Monkey god and devoted ally of Rama in the Ramayana; leapt to Lanka and carried a mountain of healing herbs.
Sanskrit epic of the Kurukshetra War between the Pandavas and Kauravas, containing the Bhagavad Gita.
Ten-headed demon king of Lanka who abducted Sita, prompting Rama's war in the Ramayana.
Hindu preserver god who takes ten avatars, including Krishna and Rama, to restore cosmic order.
Divine eagle-man mount of Vishnu, enemy of the serpent nagas.
Vedic god of fire who serves as messenger between mortals and gods through sacrificial flame.
Shared wife of the five Pandava brothers in the Mahabharata, disrobed by the Kauravas.
Avatar of Vishnu and hero of the Ramayana who rescues Sita from Ravana with Hanuman's help.
Hindu creator god of the trimurti, depicted with four faces, less widely worshipped than Vishnu or Shiva.
Hindu warrior goddess who slays the buffalo demon Mahishasura.
Hindu god of desire who was burned to ash by Shiva's third eye.
Fierce Hindu goddess of destruction and time, depicted with a necklace of skulls.
Tragic Mahabharata warrior, secretly the Pandavas' elder half-brother, loyal to the Kauravas.
Hindu goddess of wealth and fortune, consort of Vishnu.
Serpent deities of Hindu and Buddhist myth, enemies of Garuda.
Five brothers, heroes of the Mahabharata, who battle their cousins the Kauravas.
Hindu mother goddess, consort of Shiva, mother of Ganesha.
Vedic ritual drink and its deified form, associated with the moon and immortality.
Hindu sun god who rides a chariot drawn by seven horses.
Vedic god of cosmic order and the waters and oceans.
Hindu (and Buddhist) god of death and judge of the dead.
Hindu goddess personifying the Ganges River, said to descend from heaven to earth.
Hindu war god, son of Shiva and Parvati, brother of Ganesha.
Cousins and rivals of the Pandavas in the Mahabharata's Kurukshetra War.
Progenitor of humanity in Hindu myth, survivor of a great flood.
Female avatar of Vishnu who enchants demons to recover the nectar of immortality.
Cosmic mountain at the center of Hindu and Buddhist cosmology, home of the gods.
Sanskrit epic of Rama's quest to rescue Sita from Ravana with Hanuman's aid.
Sage credited as author of the Ramayana epic.
Power-seeking demon-like beings in Hindu myth, perpetual rivals of the devas.
Ten incarnations of Vishnu, including Krishna, Rama, and Narasimha, sent to restore dharma.
Mighty Pandava brother of the Mahabharata known for immense strength and appetite.
Homeland of Hindu mythology and epics like the Ramayana and Mahabharata.
Ravana's powerful son in the Ramayana, defeated by Lakshmana.
Vulture demigod of the Ramayana who fights Ravana to try to save Sita.
Final, prophesied future avatar of Vishnu, who will end the current age on a white horse.
Loyal younger brother of Rama in the Ramayana who accompanies him into exile.
Island kingdom of the demon king Ravana where Sita is held captive in the Ramayana.
Sacred flower symbol of purity, associated with Vishnu, Lakshmi, and Brahma's emergence.
Man-lion avatar of Vishnu who kills the demon Hiranyakashipu at a threshold, neither day nor night.
Wife of Rama abducted by Ravana in the Ramayana, later proves her purity by fire.
Boar avatar of Vishnu who raises the earth goddess from the cosmic ocean on his tusks.
Sage credited as compiler of the Vedas and author of the Mahabharata.
Vedic serpent demon of drought slain by Indra to release the world's waters.
Eldest Pandava brother of the Mahabharata, known for his devotion to truth and gambling loss of his kingdom.
Celestial dancing nymphs of Hindu myth who entertain the gods and test ascetics.
Section of the Mahabharata in which Krishna counsels Arjuna before battle.
Sacred Indian river personified as the goddess Ganga, said to descend from heaven.
Hindu pilgrimage festival tied to the myth of the churning of the ocean and the nectar of immortality.
Climactic eighteen-day battle of the Mahabharata between the Pandavas and Kauravas.
Serpent deities of Hindu and Buddhist myth, enemies of Garuda.
Axe-wielding avatar of Vishnu who exterminated the corrupt warrior class.
Genre of Hindu texts containing myths, genealogies, and cosmology.
Classical language of Hindu scripture and epics like the Ramayana and Mahabharata.
Hindu goddess of knowledge, music, and the arts, consort of Brahma.
Dwarf avatar of Vishnu who reclaims the cosmos from the demon king Bali in three strides.

sections.name: East Asian Mythology
sections.blurb: Chinese, Japanese, and Korean traditions: Shinto kami and Amaterasu, Chinese creation and immortal lore, and Korea's founding myth.

entries.note: Chinese creator goddess who repaired the broken sky and molded humanity from clay.
Homeland of Shinto mythology, kami spirits, and the sun goddess Amaterasu.
Japanese sun goddess who hid in a cave, plunging the world into darkness until lured out.
Homeland of the Dangun foundation myth, said to have founded the first Korean kingdom.
Homeland of myths involving Nuwa, the Jade Emperor, and the Yellow Emperor.
Group of Chinese Taoist immortals, including Lu Dongbin, each with distinctive powers and symbols.
Chinese supreme deity ruling over heaven and the Taoist pantheon.
Japanese storm god who slew the eight-headed serpent Yamata-no-Orochi and quarreled with sister Amaterasu.
Chinese archer who shot down nine of the ten suns, husband of moon goddess Chang'e.
Japanese god of nation-building and medicine who ceded rulership of Japan to Amaterasu's descendants.
Japanese dragon god of the sea who rules from a palace beneath the waves.
Shinto spirits inhabiting natural features and ancestors in Japanese belief.
Setting of the Dangun founding myth of the first Korean kingdom.
Chinese symbol of immortality, guarded in the celestial garden of the Queen Mother of the West.
Chinese Monkey King of Journey to the West with a shape-shifting cudgel and cloud-somersault.
Japanese storm god who slew Yamata-no-Orochi (variant spelling of Susanoo).
Japanese supernatural creatures and spirits, including kappa and tengu.
Japanese water-dwelling trickster spirit with a water-filled head-dish.
Tibetan warrior-king hero of the Epic of King Gesar, a lengthy Central Asian oral epic.
Full honorific title of the Japanese sun goddess Amaterasu.
Giant catfish (namazu) blamed for earthquakes in Japanese folklore, pinned down by a god.
Legendary founder of the first Korean kingdom, son of a heavenly prince and a bear-turned-woman.
Chinese archer who shot down nine suns (variant spelling of Hou Yi).
Japanese primordial creator couple who birthed the islands of Japan; Izanami died and became goddess of the underworld.
Japanese primordial creator god, husband of Izanami, who purified himself and birthed Amaterasu.
Region of Japan central to the myths of Okuninushi and the Susanoo dragon-slaying.
Region of Japan central to the myths of Okuninushi and the Susanoo dragon-slaying.
Japanese fox spirit capable of shapeshifting, often into a beautiful woman.
Japanese sacred sword found in the tail of the slain serpent Yamata-no-Orochi.
Chinese goddess of the sea, protector of sailors and fishermen.
Grandson of Amaterasu sent to rule Japan, ancestor of the imperial line.
Japanese god of thunder and storms, often paired with the wind god Fujin.
Staple crop tied to agricultural deities across East and Southeast Asian tradition.
